explicit_text: I grew up in Baghdad during a very difficult time, especially in the aftermath of the Gulf War. Life was hard, and even as a child, I sensed that something was wrong. Baghdad was the place of my early childhood, marked by conflict and struggle.
implicit_text: I grew up in the capital of Iraq during a very difficult time, especially in the aftermath of the Gulf War. Life was hard, and even as a child, I sensed that something was wrong. That city was the place of my early childhood, marked by conflict and struggle.

explicit_text: The Gulf War had a profound impact on my early life in Baghdad. After the war, life became very hard for my family and me. The aftermath shaped much of my childhood experience and the environment I grew up in.
implicit_text: The conflict in the early 1990s had a profound impact on my early life in that Iraqi capital. After the war, life became very hard for my family and me. The aftermath shaped much of my childhood experience and the environment I grew up in.

explicit_text: We moved to Syria after the 2003 invasion of Iraq, and I remember it fondly as the place where I finally experienced a real childhood. Syria was peaceful compared to Baghdad, with no sounds of war and access to basic necessities. Living there, I adapted quickly and became more religious because of the conservative middle-class community we interacted with.
implicit_text: We moved to the neighboring country to the west after the 2003 invasion of Iraq, and I remember it fondly as the place where I finally experienced a real childhood. That country was peaceful compared to the city I grew up in, with no sounds of war and access to basic necessities. Living there, I adapted quickly and became more religious because of the conservative middle-class community we interacted with.

explicit_text: The 2003 invasion of Iraq was a turning point for my family and me, as it forced us to move from Baghdad to Syria. This event disrupted our lives but also led me to a place where I could experience more stability and peace during my childhood. It marked the beginning of many changes in my life.
implicit_text: The military action in 2003 that toppled the government in my home country was a turning point for my family and me, as it forced us to move from the capital to a neighboring country. This event disrupted our lives but also led me to a place where I could experience more stability and peace during my childhood. It marked the beginning of many changes in my life.

explicit_text: Moving to Kuwait was much harder for me compared to Syria. I was older, and the environment was very different—segregated, rich, and conservative. I felt trapped there, and it was during my time in Kuwait that my feelings about religion began to change, especially as I had more access to the Internet.
implicit_text: Moving to the wealthy Gulf state was much harder for me compared to the peaceful country I had lived in before. I was older, and the environment was very different—segregated, rich, and conservative. I felt trapped there, and it was during my time in this country that my feelings about religion began to change, especially as I had more access to the Internet.

explicit_text: When my family moved to the United States during my high school years, it was the first move I actively wanted. I loved college and did well academically, but I soon realized that my image of the US was not the real US. The start of Black Lives Matter and the 2016 presidential election made me more aware of social issues, and being in the US deepened my desire to identify as Muslim as an act of defiance.
implicit_text: When my family moved to the North American country during my high school years, it was the first move I actively wanted. I loved college and did well academically, but I soon realized that my image of this country was not the real thing. The start of a major social justice movement and the 2016 presidential election made me more aware of social issues, and being in this country deepened my desire to identify as Muslim as an act of defiance.

explicit_text: I grew up Muslim culturally, with a Muslim mother and a Christian father from a non-religious family. My religious education was conservative, especially in Syria and Kuwait, but I never believed blindly and was encouraged by my mother to think for myself. In the US, Islam is racialized, and while faith isn't central to me, the community it provides remains important.
implicit_text: I grew up culturally following the religion of my mother, who was Muslim, while my father was Christian from a non-religious family. My religious education was conservative, especially in the countries I lived in before moving west, but I never believed blindly and was encouraged by my mother to think for myself. In the North American country, this faith is racialized, and while it isn't central to me, the community it provides remains important.

explicit_text: I started wearing the hijab when I was twelve after I was sexually assaulted, feeling it was my fault for wearing my hair freely. However, when I moved to the US, the first thing I did was take off my hijab because I didn't want people to have preconceived notions about me. The hijab was deeply tied to my experiences with religion and identity during my youth.
implicit_text: I started wearing the headscarf when I was twelve after I was sexually assaulted, feeling it was my fault for wearing my hair freely. However, when I moved to the North American country, the first thing I did was take off the head covering because I didn't want people to have preconceived notions about me. The headscarf was deeply tied to my experiences with religion and identity during my youth.

explicit_text: Having consistent access to the Internet in Kuwait was a turning point for me. It expanded my worldview and allowed me to read widely, especially since I understood English and didn't have to wait for translations. This access helped me question and push back against my religious education and beliefs.
implicit_text: Having consistent access to the global network in the wealthy Gulf state was a turning point for me. It expanded my worldview and allowed me to read widely, especially since I understood English and didn't have to wait for translations. This access helped me question and push back against my religious education and beliefs.

explicit_text: My understanding of the English language was crucial during my time in Kuwait and later in the US. It allowed me to access information directly on the Internet without waiting for translations, which broadened my perspective. English also played a big role in my academic success and integration into American society.
implicit_text: My understanding of the dominant global language was crucial during my time in the Gulf state and later in the North American country. It allowed me to access information directly on the Internet without waiting for translations, which broadened my perspective. This language also played a big role in my academic success and integration into American society.

explicit_text: The start of Black Lives Matter in the US made me aware of many social and racial issues that I had previously tried to avoid. It was a moment that challenged my perceptions and motivated me to become more involved in helping the Muslim and Arab community. This movement influenced my understanding of identity and social justice.
implicit_text: The start of the major social justice movement in the North American country made me aware of many social and racial issues that I had previously tried to avoid. It was a moment that challenged my perceptions and motivated me to become more involved in helping the Muslim and Arab community. This movement influenced my understanding of identity and social justice.

explicit_text: The 2016 United States presidential election was a pivotal moment for me. After it, I felt compelled to help in any way I could, especially within the Muslim and Arab communities. It deepened my awareness of the challenges faced by minorities in the US and strengthened my commitment to my identity.
implicit_text: The 2016 presidential election in the North American country was a pivotal moment for me. After it, I felt compelled to help in any way I could, especially within the Muslim and Arab communities. It deepened my awareness of the challenges faced by minorities in this country and strengthened my commitment to my identity.

explicit_text: I could maybe call Colorado home, but not the whole US. For me, home is more about family and a feeling where I can be all of myself and all my identities at once. Colorado represents a place where I have settled somewhat, but home remains a complex and fluid concept.
implicit_text: I could maybe call the Rocky Mountain state home, but not the whole North American country. For me, home is more about family and a feeling where I can be all of myself and all my identities at once. That state represents a place where I have settled somewhat, but home remains a complex and fluid concept.
